coredata.dc:title: Novel quantum dots-based assay for the determination of anti-hypertensive drugs minoxidil and timolol in different matrices
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmed, Esraa S.
Bahgat, Eman A.
Saleh, Hanaa
El Sharkasy, Mona E.
Belal, Fathalla
coredata.dc:description: The present work reports a sensitive, affordable, and ecologically friendly spectrofluorimetric method for the assessment of two antihypertensive medications, namely minoxidil and timolol. Blue-emitting sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) were generated by exposing soluble starch and thiourea to a 15-minute microwave treatment. The so- prepared nanodots displayed fluorescence at 276/430 nm with a quantum yield of 22 %. Inspection of the so-prepared nano-sensor verified their doping with nitrogen and sulfur, and their size was in the range of 4.5–9.03 nm. The proposed method was found to be rectilinear in the range of 0.20–5.0 and 2.0–30.0 µg/mL, with LOQs of 0.16 and 0.82 µg/mL for minoxidil and timolol, respectively. The developed method was employed to assess the concentrations of minoxidil and timolol in their pharmaceutical formulations, with %recoveries varying between 99.00 % and 101.94 %, and low RSD values (less than 2 %). The high sensitivity of the developed method allowed its use for timolol measurement in artificial aqueous humor, with % recoveries between 97.60 %.and 101.57 %. The study further examined how each analyte interacted with the prepared dots, leading to a quenching of their fluorescence. Additionally, an interference study was utilized to evaluate the specificity of the proposed approach through determining analyte levels in the existence of common additives, co-formulated drugs, and co-administered drugs. The analytical eco-scale, GAPI and AGREE assessment techniques were utilized to confirm the suggested method greenness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nano-sensor
Starch
Thiourea
Minoxidil
Timolol
Aqueous humor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Standard solutions of MIN and TIM
Analysis of MIN and TIM in commercial dosage forms
Analysis of TIM in artificial aqueous humor
Analysis of MIN and TIM in dosage forms
Analysis of TIM in artificial aqueous humor
Assessment of the proposed method greenness

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Alexander von Humboldt foundation, Bonn, Germany for donating the instrument used in this study (Spectrofluorometer, Agilent) to one the authors (FB).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The chemical structure of (a) Minoxidil and (b) Timolol.
Synthetic procedures of (S, N-CQDS) nano sensors.
HRTEM image (a) of S, N-CQDS, (b) FT-IR spectrum of S, N-CQDS, (c) Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy of S, N-CQDS, and (d) Zeta potential analysis of S, N-CQDS.
S,N-CQDS fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (a), and Fluorescence spectrum of S, N-CQDS upon addition various concentrations of (b1) MIN (0.20–5.0 µg/mL), and (b2) TIM (2.0 ­ 30 µg/mL).
Stern-Volmer plot of (a1) MIN, and (a2) TIM at different temperatures.

(a) A co-plot demonstrating the overlap between the S, N-CQDs' fluorescence-excitation spectra and the UV–Vis absorption spectra of MIN and TIM (5.0 µg/mL), and (b1, b2) A plot of %E against different concentrations of MIN and TIM, respectively.
The green analytical procedure index (GAPI) (a), and the analytical greenness metric (AGREE) (b) for the proposed method greenness appraisal.
Analytical performance data for the analysis of the proposed methodology.
Assay of the medications under study in raw material using the proposed and comparative methodology.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result was the mean of three independent determinations.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The figures in parentheses are the tabulated t and F values at P=0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Intra- and inter-day precision data for the assessment of MIN and TIM by the proposed methodology.
Measurement of MIN and TIM % recoveries in their dosage forms using the proposed and comparative methodology.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result was the mean of three independent determinations.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The figures enclosed in parentheses are the tabulated t and F values at P=0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tolerance limits of drugs of potential interference.
Application of the developed method for measuring TIM in artificial aqueous humor.

head.ce:title: Novel quantum dots-based assay for the determination of anti-hypertensive drugs minoxidil and timolol in different matrices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Zagazig University, Zagazig 44519, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Zagazig University, Zagazig 44519, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work reports a sensitive, affordable, and ecologically friendly spectrofluorimetric method for the assessment of two antihypertensive medications, namely minoxidil and timolol. Blue-emitting sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) were generated by exposing soluble starch and thiourea to a 15-minute microwave treatment. The so- prepared nanodots displayed fluorescence at 276/430 nm with a quantum yield of 22 %. Inspection of the so-prepared nano-sensor verified their doping with nitrogen and sulfur, and their size was in the range of 4.5–9.03 nm. The proposed method was found to be rectilinear in the range of 0.20–5.0 and 2.0–30.0 µg/mL, with LOQs of 0.16 and 0.82 µg/mL for minoxidil and timolol, respectively. The developed method was employed to assess the concentrations of minoxidil and timolol in their pharmaceutical formulations, with %recoveries varying between 99.00 % and 101.94 %, and low RSD values (less than 2 %). The high sensitivity of the developed method allowed its use for timolol measurement in artificial aqueous humor, with % recoveries between 97.60 %.and 101.57 %. The study further examined how each analyte interacted with the prepared dots, leading to a quenching of their fluorescence. Additionally, an interference study was utilized to evaluate the specificity of the proposed approach through determining analyte levels in the existence of common additives, co-formulated drugs, and co-administered drugs. The analytical eco-scale, GAPI and AGREE assessment techniques were utilized to confirm the suggested method greenness.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Minoxidil and timolol are two antihypertensive drugs, with each one having its own specific mechanism of action. Minoxidil (MIN) is a direct-acting vasodilator used orally to treat severe hypertension that does not respond to conventional therapy. In such cases, MIN needs to be co-administered along with beta blockers or methyldopa to decrease the effects of cardiac acceleration and along with a diuretic, which is often a loop diuretic, to manage edema. Additionally, MIN can be topically applied for the treatment of alopecia due to its ability to stimulate hair growth. It primarily works by causing direct peripheral vasodilation of the arterioles, producing an effect on the cardiovascular system similar to that of hydralazine [1]. Chemically, MIN (Fig. 1 a) is a 6-(1-piperidinyl)-2,4-pyrimidinediamine 3-oxide [2]. Numerous techniques were published for its analysis in dosage forms including, RP-HPLC methods [3–5], spectroscopic methods [6–8] and voltametric methods [9,10].

Timolol (TIM) is a type of non-selective beta blocker used to treat high blood pressure, glaucoma, angina pectoris, and myocardial infraction. Additionally, it is employed in the preventive management of migraines. It acts by reducing cardiac output and altering baroreceptor sensitivity [1,11]. Chemically, TIM (Fig. 1b) is 1-(t-butyl amino)-3-[(4-morpholino-1,2,5-thiadiazol-3-yl) oxy]-2-propanol [2]. Numerous reports were published to analyze TIM in dosage forms and biological fluids, including RP-HPLC methods [12–15], spectrophotometric methods [16–19] and voltametric methods [20,21]. Many of these methods require multi-step procedures, costly equipment, and are time-consuming [22,23]. For these reasons, the ultimate objective of this work is to develop a rapid alternative, selective, costly effective, and sensitive approach for the measurement of two medications in their respective pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids without prior derivatization.

Carbon quantum dots symbolize a novel class of fluorescent nanoparticles, which have a size ranging from 2 to 10 nm. These nanoparticles are extensively utilized in biosensing, cellular imaging, and as pharmaceutical biomarkers [24]. The key benefits of the proposed technique include the swift synthesis of S,N-CQDs in merely 15 min, cost-effectiveness, accessibility, eco-friendliness, and minimal toxicity. The introduction of heteroatoms such as nitrogen and sulfur into CQDs enhances their electrical, optical, and structural characteristics [25,26].

The proposed method utilizes sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs), which are obtained via a microwave-assisted process utilizing a combination of soluble starch and thiourea. The so-prepared S,N-CQDs were then adopted as fluorescent nanomaterials to quantify each of MIN and TIM. These medications cause a quantifiable decrease in the luminescence of S,N-CQDs within concentration ranges of 0.20–5.0 for MIN and 2.0–30.0 µg/mL for TIM.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Minoxidil raw material was graciously provided by AL ESRAA Pharmaceutical Optima (Badr City, Egypt), with a labeled purity of 99.89 %.
Timolol raw material was generously provided by Egyptian International Pharmaceutical Industries Co. EIPICO (10th Ramadan, Egypt), with a labeled purity of 99.29 %.
Methanol (HPLC grade) was supplied by Fisher Scientific (Belgium).
Deionized water was utilized throughout the entire work.

Britton-Robinson buffer (BRb) was produced by combining aliquots of 0.04 M acetic acid, 0.04 M boric acid, and 0.04 M phosphoric acid. Subsequently, the pH was modified using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide to get a pH range between 2.1 and 12.

Minnoxilook® a topical 5 % solution of minoxidil (Batch Number: CKJ3), manufactured by AL ESRAA Pharmaceutical Optima (Badr City, Egypt). Timolol® a sterile ophthalmic 0.5 % solution of timolol (Batch Number: 2310290) manufactured by Egyptian International Pharmaceutical Industries Co. EIPICO (10th Ramadan, Egypt. All dosage forms were obtained from the neighborhood pharmacy.

Artificial aqueous humor was prepared by mixing two portions: the first portion is a sterile solution (96 ml) containing sodium bicarbonate 2.19 mg, sodium chloride 7.44 mg, dibasic sodium phosphate 0.433 mg potassium chloride 0.395 mg, and hydrochloric acid (to modify pH). The second portion is a sterile solution (4 mL) containing glutathione (4.6 mg), dextrose (23 mg), calcium chloride dihydrate (3.85 mg), and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (5 mg).

ce:section.ce:para: Cary-Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer with Microplate Reader (model Cary WinFLR, USA) sensitivity was adjusted at 800 V. Double-beam Shimadzu Spectrophotometer (model UV-1201, Kyoto, Japan) with 1 cm quartz cells.

A water bath (model HH-S6, China) was used as a heating source. Benchtop centrifuge (model 320R, Kirchlengern, Germany). Ultrasonic bath (model WUC-D06H, Korea). Jenway pH-Meter (model 3510, UK) was utilized to modify the pH of BRB and artificial aqueous humor.

High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) (model JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (model Nicolet-iS10, Waltham, MA, USA). Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (model JSM-IT100LA, Tokyo, Japan) for elemental analysis of CQDS. Zeta potential analyzer (model SZ-100 V2, Kyoto, Japan) was employed to determine the S,N-CQDs stability after preparation.
Domestic LG microwave (model MS2042DZ, Seoul, Korea) with an output of 700 W for ignition and preparation of S,N-CQDS.

To create S,N-CQDS, 15 g of soluble starch and 3 g of thiourea were dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water and then heated in the microwave for 15 min to ensure total ignition. After allowing the residue to cool, it was mixed with 30 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 2 min, and then centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm. Filtration is performed twice using a 0.45 µm syringe filter for the removal of suspended matter. Ultimately, deionized water was utilized to get the volume up to 100 mL [27].
The S,N-CQDS standard solution was made by transferring 10 mL of the initial solution into a 100-mL volumetric flask and completing to the volume with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard solutions of MIN and TIM
ce:section.ce:para: Accurately weighed 10.0 mg of each of MIN and TIM were transferred separately into 100- mL volumetric flasks, then completing the volume with methanol to create standard solutions containing 100.0 µg/mL. For MIN, a further dilution was performed to get a working standard solution of 25.0 ug/mL. Various concentrations of each medication were obtained through serial dilutions of the stock solution. The prepared solutions had been stable for a period of 14 days when stored at 4 °C.

In the case of MIN, 0.2 mL aliquots of S,N-CQDS solution were placed into a series of 10-mL volumetric flasks. Aliquot volumes (between 0.08 and 2.0 mL) of MIN working standard solution (25.0 ug/ml) were transferred, followed by 1 mL of BRB of pH 3.2. Distilled water was used to complete the volume to the mark.

In a similar manner for TIM, 0.2 mL aliquots of S,N-CQDS solution were placed into a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks. Aliquot volumes (between 0.2 and 3.0 mL) of TIM stock solution (100.0 µg/mL) were added. The final solutions were subsequently adjusted to the proper levels using distilled water. The blank's (S,N-CQDS) fluorescence spectra were first measured at 430 nm after illumination at 276 nm. The fluorescence quenching (ΔF = F0 − F) was then recorded using the same excitation and emission wavelengths. The fluorescence quenching values (ΔF) of each drug were displayed against their respective concentrations (µg/mL) to create the calibration graphs. The data were then used to derive the linear regression equations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of MIN and TIM in commercial dosage forms
ce:section.ce:para: Minnoxilook® is a topical 5 % solution of MIN. 0.1 mL Aliquot of the topical solution was transferred into a 50-mL volumetric flask then volume was completed to the mark with methanol to attain a claimed concentration of 25 µg/mL. Several working solutions were produced by serial successive dilutions. The process outlined under Section 2.3.4 was implemented, and the data were then used to conclude the corresponding linear regression equation.

Timolol® is a sterile 0.5 % ophthalmic solution of TIM. 1.0 mL Aliquot of the sterile solution were placed into a 50-mL volumetric flask, and the volume was then filled up with methanol to attain a claimed concentration of (100 µg/mL). The working solutions were then made through serial dilutions. The process outlined under Section 2.3.4 was then implemented, and the data were then used to get the associated linear regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TIM in artificial aqueous humor
ce:section.ce:para: Into a series of 10-mL volumetric flasks, 1 mL aliquots of the prepared aqueous humor were transferred followed by aliquot volumes of TIM working solutions. After the solutions were thoroughly combined, distilled water was added to reach the desired concentrations. The process outlined in Section 2.3.4 was implemented, and the corresponding regression formula was then utilized to calculate % recoveries.

The present research provides an environmentally friendly, efficient, and affordable spectrofluorimetric method for quantifying MIN and TIM. It relies on swiftly preparing water-soluble S, N-CQDS using microwave assistance, where soluble starch provides the carbon and thiourea provides nitrogen and sulfur. The attainment of a brown-colored solution signifies the end of the reaction and the complete formation of the fluorescent probe (Fig. 2 ). Following preparation, the prepared S,N-CQDS were characterized using several methodologies. HRTEM was employed to visualize the atomic arrangement of the prepared dots. The obtained image represents spherical S,N-CQDS nanoparticles with sizes in the range of 4.53–9.03 nm (Fig. 3 a). FT-IR spectroscopy was additionally employed to figure out the functional groups present in the fluorescent probe. The broad stretching band observed within the range of 3100–3300 cm−1 demonstrates the existence of OH and NH groups [28], while the stretching band at 1619 cm−1 corresponds to the CC group. CH and CN stretching bands emerged at 2925 cm−1 and 2055 cm−1 respectively. Moreover, the vibration observed at 1012 cm−1 corresponds to the C=S group [28] as illustrated in (Fig. 3b). The elemental analysis was conducted utilizing the Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) technique, disclosing the composition as follows: carbon (C) constituted 46.39 % of the mass, oxygen (O) accounted for 38.03 %, nitrogen comprised 15.49 %, and Sulphur (S) constituted 0.08 % (Fig. 3c). The stability of the fluorescent probe was also analyzed using Zeta potential technique. The zeta potential was measured and found to be −8.94 mV (Fig. 3d), indicating that, the synthesized dots are stable, and this may be due to the repellent forces between the negatively charged functional groups over the quantum dots surface, thus preventing their agglomeration. The synthesized dots UV–VIS absorption spectrum is presented in Fig. 1S, which shows a maximum UV-absorbance at 200 nm. The spectrofluorimetric technique was then used to identify S,N-CQDS excitation and emission wavelengths (Fig. 4 a). Each of MIN and TIM has an apparent quenching impact on S,N-CQDS, as shown in Fig. 4b.

Quantum yield is a measure of the efficiency with which a particle emits photons after absorbing electromagnetic radiation. The quantum yield of the synthesized S,N-CQDs was found to be 0.22. The Single- point technique was utilized to calculate the quantum yield by applying the following Eq. (1) formula [29]:
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ
st
×
I x
/
I
st
×
η x
/
η
st
×
A
st
/
A x
where: Φx refers to the quantum yield of the unknown solution, whereas Φst is the quantum yield of the standard. I is the integrated emission intensities of the unknown and standard solutions under identical conditions, A stands for the UV-absorbance and η for the solvent's refractive index. ηx/ηst in aqueous solutions equals 1. The standard fluorescent material was quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid. Qx is 0.54 [29].

The diminution of the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDS could be caused by a number of different quenching mechanisms, including static quenching, which occurs when quenchers form a complex with fluorescent probes at the ground state before excitation (binding). Conversely, dynamic quenching happens through collisions between the quencher and the fluorescent molecule's excited state. Other significant quenching mechanisms include energy transfer and the inner filter effect (IFE) [30]. The quenching mechanism of each of MIN and TIM could be elucidated through the application of Stern Volmer’s Eq. (2) [31]:
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 + Ksv
Q
where: F0 is S,N-CQDS's fluorescence intensity in the absence of a quencher, while F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of a quencher. [Q] refers to the quencher concentration, and Ksv is the Stern Volmer quenching constant.

The Stern Volmer graphs of each MIN and TIM were constructed between F0/F and [Q] at different temperatures: 303 K, 313 K, 323 K, 333 K (Fig. 5 ). The plots were linear, and Ksv was calculated for each drug. For MIN, by increasing temperature, particle mobility and energy transfer increase, resulting in an increase in Ksv indicating a dynamic quenching mechanism. For TIM, the complex chemical stability diminished with increasing temperature, suggesting a static quenching mechanism, and this behavior was reflected in a fall in Ksv value.

The UV–VIS spectra of each of MIN and TIM were also recorded and an overlap was found between the excitation spectra of S, N-CQDS and the UV–VIS spectra of both drugs indicating that IFE can be an additional mechanism of quenching for both drugs as shown in Fig. 6 a [32]. The correction of S, N-CQDS fluorescence intensity for potential (IFE) was examined by adding increased concentrations of MIN and TIM and the calculation of corrected fluorescence intensity using the subsequent Eq. (3):
(3)
F corr
=
F obs
×
10
Aex + Aem
/ 2
where: Fcorr is the corrected fluorescence intensity, after removing IFE from Fobs, Fobs is the observed fluorescence intensity while Aex and Aem represent the absorbance of the studied drugs at both the excitation and emission wavelengths of S,N-CQDS.

The suppressed efficiency (%E) was calculated for the observed and corrected fluorescence intensities of the studied drugs utilizing the following Eq. (4):
(4)
% E = [ 1 - ( F / F 0 ) ] × 100
%E values of both the corrected and observed fluorescence intensities of MIN and TIM were plotted against the drug concentrations (Fig. 6b1& Fig. 6b2) which demonstrate that the quoted drug's ability to quench the fluorescence intensity of S, N-CQDs is significantly influenced by IFE.

Britton Robinson buffer was utilized to scrutinize the impact of pH on quenching the S,N-CQDS's fluorescence intensity by the drugs under study over the pH range of 2.1–12. For MIN, it was noticed that pH 3.2 was the most suitable value, while for TIM, it was noticed that the fluorescence intensity was not significantly altered over the entire pH range (Fig. 2S).
After testing a range of buffer volumes from 0.2 mL to 2.5 mL, it was found that utilizing 1 mL of BRb at pH 3.2 was the optimum one to achieve the greatest quenching effect in the case of MIN.

MIN and TIM were added to S,N-CQDS at intervals of one to thirty minutes; the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded; and it was noticed that the entire reaction was instantaneous (took less than one minute), and the fluorescence signals were stable for at least 30 min. These are two important advantages of this approach (Fig. 3S).
The suggested method validity was confirmed through the use of ICHQ2(R2) guidelines [33].

The suggested method's linearity was assessed through graphing fluorescence quenching values ΔF against studied drug concentrations in µg/mL to construct calibration graphs. The linear range was estimated to be 0.20–5.0 µg/mL for MIN and 2.0–30.0 µg/mL for TIM; equations (5) & (6) represent the linear regressions of MIN and TIM, respectively.
(5)
Δ
F
F 0
- F
= 89.11 C + 20.85
(6)
Δ
F
F 0
- F
= 9.72 C + 23.05

In which: F0 is S,N-CQDS's fluorescence intensity in the absence of quencher, while F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of studied drugs and C is the drug concentration measured in µg/mL. Correlation coefficients (r) approaching unity imply adequate linearity of the proposed methodology, as indicated in (Table 1 ).

The limit of detection (LOD) is the minimum concentration at which a drug can be identified; statistically, it is calculated as 3.3 Sa/b, whereas the limit of quantitation (LOQ) is the minimum concentration at which a drug can be quantitatively measured, and it is equal to 10 Sa/b, Sa is the standard deviation of the intercept, and b is the slope of the calibration curve. The values of LOD and LOQ in (Table 1) prove the adequate sensitivity of the suggested strategy.

Investigations of the suggested method selectivity revealed that common excipients in the MIN topical solution Minnoxilook®, had no effect on the signal of fluorescence produced by this reaction. Conversely, the analysis of TIM in commercial eye drops Timolol®, reveals interference caused by the matrix. The effect of the matrix on the recorded (ΔF) values was eliminated using the standard addition calibration technique. The method has been performed by measuring the fluorescence quenching values (ΔF) of TIM eye drop at concentrations of 5.0, 10.0, and 15.0 µg/mL, and then measuring ΔF values of eye drop with varying volumes of standard solution containing 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, and 15.0 µg/mL. A calibration plot was then established by graphing the fluorescence quenching values versus the concentration of the standard solution added. The % recoveries of both drugs were then calculated as displayed in Table 4 .

The tolerance limits were also evaluated for the drugs that are frequently co-formulated with TIM, such as brimonidine, travoprost, and latanoprost, for the effective treatment of ocular hypertension. The results displayed in Table 5 represent drug concentrations that cause a 5 % relative standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of MIN and TIM in dosage forms
ce:section.ce:para: The suggested approach was successfully used to assay MIN and TIM in their commercial topical solutions and eye drops. The suggested method was evaluated by contrasting the data generated by the proposed technique with the data acquired by the comparison approach [7,18]. The findings were compared using the student t-test and the Variance Ratio F-test, as shown in Table 4. The tabulated results of the t-test and F-test show that there was no discernible variation between the two approaches [34] illustrating that the proposed approach was sufficiently accurate and precise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TIM in artificial aqueous humor
ce:section.ce:para:
The maximum concentration of TIM in aqueous humor (Cmax) is about 1.0 µg/mL [35] which lies within the working concentration range of the proposed method,therefore, it could be used for the drug measurement in aqueous humor. Samples were prepared as stated in Section 2.3.6 and a calibration plot was generated by plotting ΔF values against the corresponding drug concentration (µg/mL). The regression equation was then computed as follows:
Δ F
F 0
- F
= 8.77 C + 27.87
r = 0.9997
where: F0 is S,N-CQDS's fluorescence intensity in the absence of quencher, while F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of studied medications, and C is the drug concentration in µg/mL and r is the correlation coefficient. % Recoveries were then calculated for the studied drug and ranged from 97.60 to 101.57 % as shown in Table 6 and Fig. 4S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the proposed method greenness
ce:section.ce:para: The central goal of green analytical chemistry is to enhance the environmental friendliness and safety of analytical procedures. This evaluation considers several factors, such a, the quantity and harmfulness of the chemicals used, waste output and its proper disposal, energy requirements, methods of sample preparation, and the level of automation. The environmental sustainability of the suggested approach was assessed through multiple established approaches. The analytical Eco-Scale [36], which relies on calculating the penalty points, and deducting the outcome from the initial value of 100 points. A greater subtracted value indicates that the analytical approach is very green. Penalty points of the analytical procedures are assigned to the quantity of reagent and its possible hazards, energy needs, occupational hazards, and the amount of waste and its treatment. The analytical Eco-Scale total score of the suggested method is equal to 82, which means excellent green analysis, as indicated in (Table 1S). The green analytical procedure index (GAPI) [37]; is another green appraisal technique that uses a pictogram and a color scale with three rating levels for each step to categorize how green an analytical technique is. The pictogram is divided into five pentagrams, which assess sample preparation, transport, amount of reagent used and its health and environmental hazards, instrument energy input, and amount of waste and its treatment. The evaluation is performed by giving each assigned step a specific color (green, yellow, or red) depending on its environmental outcomes, whether minimum, medium, or significant. The proposed method GAPI assessment results are indicated in (Fig. 7 a). Green and yellow pentagrams made up most of the pictograms with only two red pentagrams. Two red pentagrams at sections (1–15) were due to the off-line collection of samples, and no waste treatment. The previous explanations affirmed that the proposed technique is environmentally friendly. The analytical greenness metric approach (AGREE) [38], is an additional technique for the assessment of greenness. It is characterized by being flexible, comprehensive, instructive, and easy to understand outcome. It is a clock-like graph divided into 12 pieces, representing the 12 significant principles of green analytical chemistry, including sample preparation, reagent volume and toxicity, energy input, automation, and miniaturization. The result is presented in the middle in the range of 0–1 scale. The AGREE assessment result of the proposed method is equal to 0.65 with only one red section, which indicates that the proposed method is eco-friendly (Fig. 7b).

A novel quantum dots-based assay was successfully developed and applied for the quantitation of each of MIN and TIM in pharmaceutical preparations and biological fluids. The suggested approach relies on the high fluorescence intensity of the so-prepared S, N-CQDS that is quenched by the two drugs. The water-soluble S,N-CQDS were generated through the use of soluble starch as a carbon source and thiourea as a sulfur and nitrogen source. The generated nano-sensor has a particle size in the range of 4.53–9.03 nm. The proposed technique is distinguished by its rapid production in only 15 min, being inexpensive, affordable, environmentally friendly, and having low toxicity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Alexander von Humboldt foundation, Bonn, Germany for donating the instrument used in this study (Spectrofluorometer, Agilent) to one the authors (FB).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125141.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A. Brayfield, Martindale, The Complete Drug Reference, 36th ed., The Pharmaceutical Press, London, 2009, pp. 1342–1412.
The British Pharmacopoeia, British Pharmacopoeia Commission, vol. 317, The Stationary Office, London, 2020, pp. 1145–1147.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Developement and validation of analytical method for simultaneous determination of minoxidil and finasteride in pharmaceutical dosage form by RP-HPLC method
Int. J. Pharma. Sci. Res.

Annales Pharmaceutiques Françaises
